children.name: Didelphis
children.description: Large opossums — the most widespread and familiar opossum genus, including the Virginia opossum, North America's only marsupial. Large opossums are highly opportunistic omnivores, eating insects, small vertebrates, fruit, carrion, and refuse. The Virginia opossum's extraordinary immune system makes it uniquely resistant to pit viper venom — a peptide called LTNF in its blood neutralises the toxic phospholipase enzymes in rattlesnake and cottonmouth venom; researchers have synthesised this peptide as a potential broad-spectrum antivenom. 'Playing possum' — feigning death when severely threatened — is an involuntary neurological response rather than a conscious strategy; the animal's body temperature drops, a foul smell is produced, and it may remain motionless for up to four hours.

children.name: Didelphis virginiana
children.description: North America's only marsupial. Immune to the venom of most North American pit vipers. Naturally resistant to rabies (body temperature too low for virus to survive). Range has expanded significantly northward in the 20th century.

children.name: Didelphis marsupialis
children.description: The most widespread opossum in South America. Distinguished from the Virginia opossum by a black-eared face. Found from Mexico to Bolivia in tropical and subtropical forest, often near human habitation.

children.name: Didelphis albiventris
children.description: The dominant large opossum of the Brazilian cerrado, Chaco, and Atlantic Forest, recognisable by its white ears and pale underparts. It is highly adaptable and thrives in fragmented and degraded habitats across much of south-central South America from Brazil to Argentina and Uruguay. Like its relatives, it produces large litters and young complete development in the pouch, making it one of the region's most common medium-sized mammals.

A large opossum of open and semi-open habitats from Brazil to Argentina, recognisable by its distinctive white ears. It inhabits grasslands, cerrado, and forest edges, and is notably tolerant of human-modified landscapes including pastures and suburban gardens. Like other large opossums it has partial resistance to pit viper venom, an adaptation well-studied in the populations of the Brazilian Cerrado.

children.name: Didelphis aurita
children.description: A large opossum restricted to the Atlantic Forest of eastern Brazil, from Bahia to Rio Grande do Sul, and extending into Paraguay and north-eastern Argentina. It was long confused with the White-eared Opossum but is typically larger, darker, and has conspicuously larger, black-tipped ears. It inhabits forest interiors, edges, and secondary growth and is an important seed disperser and frugivore in the threatened Atlantic Forest biome.

children.name: Didelphis pernigra
children.description: A large opossum of the Andes from Venezuela and Colombia south to Bolivia and north-western Argentina, found at elevations from 400 to 3,000 m. It has a distinctive dark dorsal stripe and white ears, and is the highest-altitude opossum on the continent. It inhabits montane cloud forests and forest edges, and has a slower reproductive rate than lowland species, an adaptation common to high-altitude marsupials.

A large, black-eared opossum endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil and adjacent Argentina and Paraguay. It is an important seed disperser and frugivore in one of the world's most threatened forest biomes. Closely resembles Didelphis marsupialis but is confined to Atlantic Forest and distinguishable by genetic and morphological characters.

children.name: Didelphis imperfecta
children.description: Restricted to the Guiana Shield region — Venezuela, Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana, and adjacent Brazil — this recently recognised species was long treated as a subspecies of the White-eared Opossum. It inhabits tropical lowland rainforest and forest edges, and is distinguished from other Didelphis by its geographic range and subtle morphological differences confirmed by molecular studies.

A medium-sized Didelphis of the Guiana Highlands and adjacent Venezuela and Brazil, previously treated as a subspecies of D. albiventris. It occupies savanna-forest mosaics and is distinguished by cranial and pelage characters from its congeners. Relatively little studied due to its remote range in the tepui foothills.

children.name: Didelphis pernigra
children.description: An Andean large opossum ranging from Colombia and Venezuela south through Peru and Bolivia, found at elevations up to 3,500 m. It was long subsumed within D. albiventris and elevated to species status on the basis of geographic isolation and morphology. Its tolerance of cold high-altitude environments makes it physiologically notable among the large opossums.

children.name: Philander
children.description: Four-eyed opossums — medium-sized, agile opossums named for the distinctive white spots above each eye that resemble a second pair of eyes, a possible anti-predator adaptation. There are around nine species, all restricted to the Neotropics. They are largely arboreal to semi-arboreal and strongly associated with humid forest. Females have a well-developed pouch. Four-eyed opossums are dietary generalists, taking insects, small vertebrates, fruit, and seeds.

children.name: Philander opossum
children.description: The most widespread four-eyed opossum, ranging from Mexico to northern Argentina in tropical and subtropical forests. It is a skilled climber and swimmer, often foraging along forest streams. The white supraorbital spots are a characteristic feature shared by all members of the genus.

children.name: Philander frenatus
children.description: An Atlantic Forest endemic ranging through southeastern Brazil, eastern Paraguay, and northeastern Argentina. It is one of the more frequently encountered marsupials in the Atlantic Forest, studied intensively for its role in seed dispersal and small mammal community ecology. The species name refers to the stripe pattern on the face.

children.name: Philander nigratus
children.description: A four-eyed opossum of the western and central Amazon, characterised by unusually dark dorsal pelage compared to other members of the genus. It was formally described from Amazonas state, Brazil, and occupies primary and secondary lowland rainforest. Its ecology is poorly documented but it is presumed to be a dietary generalist like its congeners.

children.name: Philander quica
children.description: Found in the eastern Amazon of Brazil and the Guiana Shield, this species was long confused with P. opossum before being separated on molecular and morphological grounds. It inhabits humid lowland forest and is nocturnal and primarily arboreal. The common name 'quica' is a local Brazilian term for small opossums.

children.name: Lutreolina
children.description: Lutrine opossums or thick-tailed opossums — robust, weasel-like opossums with a muscular build, a thick tail, and short limbs adapted for a more terrestrial, predatory lifestyle. The genus contains one or two species depending on the authority. Lutreolina crassicaudata is an active predator of fish, amphibians, and small mammals, and is unusual among opossums in its highly carnivorous diet. It occupies grasslands, marshes, and riparian habitats in southern South America.

children.name: Lutreolina crassicaudata
children.description: A weasel-shaped opossum of open and riverine habitats in Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay, and southern Brazil. It is among the most carnivorous of all opossums, actively hunting fish, frogs, lizards, and small mammals. Its thick, muscular tail and dense waterproof fur reflect adaptations to semi-aquatic hunting in marshes and stream margins.

children.name: Lutreolina massoia
children.description: Described from Argentina in 2009, this recently recognised species was previously lumped with L. crassicaudata. It differs in cranial dimensions, dental morphology, and geographic range. Named in honour of Argentine mammalogist Elio Massoia, a major contributor to South American small mammal taxonomy.

children.name: Metachirus
children.description: The brown four-eyed opossum — a monotypic genus of medium-sized, terrestrial opossum without a pouch, instead carrying young clinging to its back. Unlike the similarly spotted Philander, Metachirus lacks a pouch even in females, and the young cling exposed to the ventral fur. It is a ground-dwelling insectivore and frugivore of Neotropical lowland forest, notable for its unusually long hind legs and rapid terrestrial locomotion.

children.name: Metachirus nudicaudatus
children.description: The sole member of Metachirus, ranging from Nicaragua to northern Argentina in humid lowland and montane forests. It is unusually long-legged for an opossum and is primarily terrestrial, foraging in leaf litter for invertebrates and fallen fruit. Females lack a pouch; newborns attach to exposed abdominal nipples and are later carried on the mother's back.

children.name: Marmosa
children.description: Mouse opossums — small, agile opossums without a permanent pouch; their young attach directly to nipples on the mother's bare belly and are later carried clinging to her fur. There are over 20 species of Marmosa, ranging from 20 to 300 grams. They are important pollinators of tropical flowers, feeding extensively on nectar and pollen in a mouse-like fashion that gets nectar on their fur. Mouse opossums are partially arboreal and use their prehensile tail as a fifth limb. They enter daily torpor to conserve energy, and can survive periods of food scarcity by substantially reducing their metabolic rate.

children.name: Marmosa robinsoni
children.description: A widespread and common species from Panama to Venezuela. An important nocturnal pollinator of tropical flowers. Can enter torpor during food scarcity. Females lack a pouch; young cling to exposed nipples.

children.name: Marmosa murina
children.description: One of the most widespread mouse opossums in South America, ranging from Trinidad and Venezuela south through the Amazon basin to Bolivia and Brazil. It inhabits tropical rainforest, secondary growth, and forest edges, typically sleeping by day in self-made nests of leaves. An important nectar feeder and pollinator, it visits flowers of many tropical plants including Quararibea (monkey pot) trees at night.

children.name: Marmosa mexicana
children.description: Ranging from Mexico south through Central America to Panama, this is one of the most common mouse opossums in Mesoamerica. It inhabits tropical and subtropical forests, coffee plantations, and secondary growth from sea level to around 2,500 m. Nocturnal and omnivorous, it feeds on insects, fruit, and small vertebrates, and its prehensile tail allows it to forage effectively in the forest canopy.

One of the most common and widespread mouse opossums of lowland tropical South America, ranging from Colombia and Venezuela to Brazil and Bolivia. It occupies a wide range of forest types from terra firme to secondary and gallery forest. Its mousy appearance and small size led to the species epithet murina, meaning 'mouse-like'.

children.name: Marmosa lepida
children.description: One of the smallest mouse opossums, weighing around 10–30 g, with distinctly rufous or orange-brown upperparts that distinguish it from other Amazonian species. It inhabits the lowland rainforests of the Amazon basin from Colombia and Venezuela to Peru and Brazil. Its small size allows it to exploit nest cavities and crevices inaccessible to larger marsupials, and it is an important predator of insects in the forest understorey.

children.name: Marmosa rubra
children.description: A medium-sized mouse opossum with strikingly reddish-brown upperparts, found in the western Amazon basin of Ecuador, Peru, and Colombia. It inhabits lowland tropical rainforest, particularly in terra firme (non-flooded) forest, and is primarily nocturnal and insectivorous. It was long poorly known, but camera trap surveys have revealed it to be more widespread in western Amazonia than previously thought.

children.name: Marmosa constantiae
children.description: A relatively large mouse opossum with woolly fur and pale underparts, found in the Chaco, cerrado, and adjacent habitats of Bolivia, Paraguay, and Brazil. It is one of the more robust members of the genus and has large eyes adapted to its strictly nocturnal lifestyle. Like other mouse opossums it is an important consumer of fruit and insects in the dry and transitional forests it inhabits.

children.name: Marmosa paraguayana
children.description: A forest-dependent mouse opossum of the Atlantic Forest biome in south-eastern South America, found in south-eastern Brazil, Paraguay, and north-eastern Argentina. It inhabits dense humid forest and forest edges and is threatened by the severe ongoing fragmentation of the Atlantic Forest. It feeds on invertebrates, fruit, and nectar, and is considered a useful indicator of Atlantic Forest health.

children.name: Marmosa alstoni
children.description: Found in lowland tropical forest from southern Mexico through Central America, named after the British zoologist Edward Richard Alston who described many Central American mammals in the 19th century. It inhabits the forest floor and lower strata of humid lowland and foothill forest, and is an agile climber despite spending more time on the ground than most mouse opossums. It is nocturnal and primarily insectivorous.

children.name: Marmosa germana
children.description: A small mouse opossum of north-western South America, found in lowland tropical forests of Ecuador and Peru west of the Andes. It is morphologically similar to several related species and its taxonomy has been revised multiple times with molecular data. Like its congeners it is an agile nocturnal forager that uses its prehensile tail to navigate the forest canopy and understorey in search of insects and ripe fruit.

A small, brightly coloured mouse opossum with striking rufous dorsal fur, found in the Amazon basin from Peru and Bolivia east to Brazil and the Guianas. It is among the smallest Marmosa species. It inhabits humid lowland forest and forest edges, foraging in the understorey for insects and soft fruit.

children.name: Marmosa mexicana
children.description: The northernmost mouse opossum, ranging from Tamaulipas in northeastern Mexico through Central America to Panama. It inhabits humid montane and lowland forests and is one of the few Marmosa species found north of South America. It is nocturnal and arboreal, feeding on insects, small lizards, and fruit.

children.name: Marmosa xerophila
children.description: A xeric-adapted mouse opossum of the dry Caribbean lowlands of Colombia and Venezuela, uniquely among Marmosa associated with arid scrub and thorn woodland rather than humid forest. It is listed as Vulnerable due to habitat loss in the highly degraded Caribbean coastal plain. Its pale coloration and reduced fur density are adaptations to a hot, dry environment.

children.name: Marmosa waterhousei
children.description: A medium-sized mouse opossum of Trinidad and northeastern Venezuela, named in honour of the English naturalist George Robert Waterhouse. It was one of the first New World marsupials studied by British naturalists in the nineteenth century. It occupies humid lowland forest and forest edges in its limited coastal and insular range.

children.name: Monodelphis
children.description: Short-tailed opossums — the largest opossum genus with over 20 species, and the only opossums regularly active by day. Unlike most opossums, they lack a prehensile tail. The grey short-tailed opossum (Monodelphis domestica) is the most important research marsupial in the world — its genome was sequenced in 2007, revealing the largest genome of any known marsupial. It is used in studies of cancer (it can be induced to develop melanoma by UV exposure), neurological development, and reproductive immunology. Short-tailed opossums are also notable for producing milk that changes dramatically in composition as the young develop.

children.name: Monodelphis domestica
children.description: The most important research marsupial. Genome sequenced in 2007 — its 3.6 Gb genome is the largest marsupial genome known. Can develop melanoma under UV exposure, making it a model for human skin cancer research.

children.name: Monodelphis americana
children.description: Recognised by three distinct dark dorsal stripes running from the nape to the base of the tail, this diminutive opossum inhabits the Atlantic Forest and cerrado of eastern Brazil. It is one of the few marsupials regularly active during the day, foraging on the forest floor for insects, small lizards, and fruit. It is an important prey item for snakes and small raptors in its range.

children.name: Monodelphis brevicaudata
children.description: A robust short-tailed opossum with strikingly reddish-orange flanks and legs contrasting with darker upperparts, found from Venezuela and Trinidad south through Guyana and northern Brazil. It inhabits tropical lowland forest, second growth, and savannah edges. More terrestrial than most opossums, it forages actively on the forest floor during both day and night for invertebrates and small vertebrates.

A robust, handsomely marked short-tailed opossum of the Guiana Shield and adjacent Amazon basin, characterised by rufous flanks and legs contrasting with a grey dorsum. It is diurnal and terrestrial, foraging in leaf litter and on the forest floor for invertebrates and small vertebrates. It is one of the largest Monodelphis species.

children.name: Monodelphis emiliae
children.description: A small short-tailed opossum of the Amazon basin, found in Brazil, Peru, and Bolivia, inhabiting humid lowland rainforest and seasonally flooded habitats. It is distinguished from related species by its pale orange-brown colouration and smaller size. It is among the least-studied members of the genus, but is believed to be primarily insectivorous and diurnally active like other members of Monodelphis.

A boldly tricoloured short-tailed opossum of the central and eastern Amazon of Brazil, with contrasting dark dorsum, bright rufous sides, and pale venter. It inhabits primary and secondary lowland rainforest and is one of the more strikingly patterned members of the genus. Species named for the Brazilian state of Pará where early specimens were collected.

children.name: Monodelphis americana
children.description: A small, distinctively patterned short-tailed opossum of eastern Brazil's Atlantic Forest and adjacent Caatinga, with three dark dorsal stripes running along the back. It is a ground-dweller, active by day and capable of high population densities in secondary forest and agricultural margins. Among the most frequently trapped small marsupials in the Atlantic Forest.

children.name: Monodelphis dimidiata
children.description: An inhabitant of the grasslands, marshes, and scrubby habitats of south-eastern Brazil, Uruguay, and Argentina, this is the most southerly-distributed short-tailed opossum. Unusually among marsupials, males die shortly after the breeding season, giving the species an annual semelparous life history similar to some Australian marsupials. This post-reproductive male die-off ensures that the entire effort of the breeding season is channelled into the offspring.

children.name: Monodelphis sorex
children.description: A tiny, shrew-like short-tailed opossum of the Atlantic Forest in south-eastern Brazil, named for its resemblance to shrews in size and pointed snout. One of the smaller members of the genus, it weighs only 10–25 g and is a highly active predator of insects, spiders, and other small invertebrates in the leaf litter and forest floor of humid coastal forest.

children.name: Monodelphis theresa
children.description: A poorly known short-tailed opossum from south-eastern Brazil, found in the Atlantic Forest of Minas Gerais, Rio de Janeiro, and adjacent states. It inhabits dense humid forest understoreys and is most frequently recorded in primary and well-recovered secondary forest. Like other short-tailed opossums it is a solitary, territorial predator of invertebrates that ventures out by both day and night.

children.name: Monodelphis scalops
children.description: Distinguished by an elongated, pointed snout reminiscent of a mole, this specialised short-tailed opossum inhabits the Atlantic Forest and adjacent forests of south-eastern Brazil. Its long rostrum may be an adaptation for probing soil and leaf litter to locate earthworms and soil invertebrates, a foraging niche unusual among marsupials. It is considered vulnerable to habitat loss in the highly fragmented Atlantic Forest.

A small short-tailed opossum of the grasslands and pampas of southern Brazil, Uruguay, and northeastern Argentina. Unlike most congeners of humid forest, it is well adapted to open, seasonally dry habitats. Males die after their single breeding season — one of the few semelparity examples in marsupials, analogous to the die-off seen in some dasyurids.

children.name: Monodelphis adusta
children.description: A uniformly dark brown short-tailed opossum of the Andean foothills and adjacent lowlands from Colombia to Bolivia. Its sepia coloration is unusual among a genus with many striped or bicoloured forms. It occupies montane and foothill forests at elevations from 500 to 2,000 m.

children.name: Monodelphis kunsi
children.description: A tiny short-tailed opossum — among the smallest marsupials — from Bolivia and adjacent Brazil and Argentina, named in honour of American mammalogist Philip Myers' collaborator Karl Kuns. It inhabits dry forest, cerrado, and chaco scrub, and is one of the least-studied Monodelphis species due to its cryptic habits and small size.

children.name: Monodelphis osgoodi
children.description: A small, poorly known short-tailed opossum from the eastern Andes of Bolivia and Peru. Named in honour of the American mammalogist Wilfred Hudson Osgood, who made major contributions to knowledge of South American small mammals. It inhabits humid montane forest and is rarely collected.

children.name: Monodelphis touan
children.description: A small, bicoloured short-tailed opossum of the Guiana Shield and lower Amazon of Brazil, French Guiana, Suriname, and Guyana. It inhabits primary lowland rainforest and is one of several morphologically similar small Monodelphis that have been teased apart through molecular systematics in recent decades. The name touan derives from a Tupi word for small animal.

children.name: Monodelphis unistriata
children.description: A small, rarely collected short-tailed opossum of the Atlantic Forest in south-eastern Brazil, named for a single dark dorsal stripe running from the crown to the base of the tail. Known from only a handful of specimens, it is one of the least-understood South American marsupials. It inhabits dense undergrowth in humid coastal forest and is presumed to be an insectivore active at ground level.

A rare, single-striped short-tailed opossum known from very few specimens collected in São Paulo and Minas Gerais states in southeastern Brazil. It inhabits Atlantic Forest remnants and is among the least-known marsupials in South America. Its conservation status is poorly evaluated due to the paucity of records.

children.name: Thylamys
children.description: Fat-tailed mouse opossums — small, big-eyed opossums characterised by the ability to store fat in their tail during periods of food abundance, drawing on these reserves during cold seasons or drought. The fattened tail is an obvious visual cue to nutritional condition and has been extensively studied as a model of energy storage in marsupials. There are around ten species distributed mainly in open and semi-arid habitats of the southern cone of South America, from the high Andes to the Patagonian steppe, with some taxa reaching the Chaco and cerrado.

children.name: Thylamys elegans
children.description: The best-studied fat-tailed opossum, widespread in the shrubland and woodland of central Chile and adjacent Argentina. It is a model organism for studying marsupial energy storage: individuals accumulate fat in the tail before winter, and tail girth is used as a direct measure of body condition. It is nocturnal, semi-arboreal, and omnivorous.

children.name: Thylamys pallidior
children.description: A pale, white-bellied fat-tailed opossum of the Patagonian steppe, Monte desert, and Andean foothills of Argentina and adjacent Chile. It is well-adapted to cold, arid environments, and its pale coloration blends with the grey-brown desert scrub. One of the southernmost marsupials, surviving winters at latitudes approaching 50°S.

children.name: Thylamys pusillus
children.description: A small fat-tailed opossum of the dry Chaco of Bolivia, Paraguay, and northern Argentina. It is the most common Thylamys in the low-altitude Gran Chaco, inhabiting thorn scrub and dry deciduous forest. It enters torpor during dry season food shortages, drawing on fat reserves stored in its thickened tail.

children.name: Thylamys venustus
children.description: A montane fat-tailed opossum of the Bolivian and Argentine Andes, ranging from about 1,500 to 3,500 m elevation in dry inter-Andean valleys and Yungas forest edges. Its buff-yellow ventral colouring distinguishes it from the white-bellied T. pallidior that overlaps with it at lower elevations. It is listed as Near Threatened due to restricted range.

children.name: Thylamys macrurus
children.description: A relatively large fat-tailed opossum of southeastern Brazil, Paraguay, and northeastern Argentina, notable for its proportionally long tail. It occupies cerrado, dry forest, and forest-grassland transition zones. The species was long considered rare but systematic surveys have located stable populations in protected cerrado.

children.name: Marmosops
children.description: Slender mouse opossums — a genus of around twelve small, narrow-bodied opossums closely related to Marmosa but typically more slender in build and more strongly associated with humid forest. They are nocturnal, arboreal insectivores and frugivores, and most species are restricted to the Amazon basin, Atlantic Forest, or Andean foothills. The genus was separated from Marmosa based on morphological and molecular evidence, and several species have been described since 2000.

children.name: Marmosops incanus
children.description: The most commonly encountered Marmosops in the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, found in forest remnants from Bahia to Rio Grande do Sul. It is nocturnal, arboreal, and feeds on insects, fruit, and nectar. It has become a key indicator species for Atlantic Forest small mammal monitoring programmes.

children.name: Marmosops ocellatus
children.description: A medium-sized slender mouse opossum of eastern Bolivia, western Brazil (Mato Grosso), and adjacent Paraguay, associated with dry forest and cerrado. It is distinguished by prominent eye-rings and a relatively large body size for the genus. It is one of the more terrestrial Marmosops, often captured in pitfall traps set at ground level.

children.name: Marmosops impavidus
children.description: A widespread slender mouse opossum of the Andean foothills and adjacent lowlands of Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia, and Venezuela. It occurs from near sea level to about 1,800 m elevation in humid forest. The species epithet means 'fearless', reportedly reflecting the animal's bold behaviour when handled.

children.name: Marmosops parvidens
children.description: A small slender mouse opossum of the Guiana Shield and lower Amazon, named for its small teeth. It inhabits primary lowland rainforest and has been recorded across French Guiana, Suriname, Guyana, Venezuela, and northern Brazil. It is one of the most frequently captured small marsupials in live-trap surveys of Guianan forests.

children.name: Gracilinanus
children.description: Gracile mouse opossums — diminutive, large-eyed opossums characterised by their delicate build, very long tails, and large, protruding eyes. They are among the smallest opossums, with some species weighing under 10 grams. Gracile mouse opossums are highly arboreal and nocturnal, foraging in the forest canopy and understorey for insects and nectar. The genus is distributed from Mexico to Argentina, with greatest diversity in South America. Several species were described or elevated from synonymy in the 1990s following molecular and morphometric reassessment.

children.name: Gracilinanus agilis
children.description: The most widespread gracile mouse opossum, found across central Brazil, Bolivia, Paraguay, and northern Argentina in cerrado, chaco, and gallery forest. It is highly agile and arboreal, with a tail much longer than its body used for balance and grasping. A prolific breeder, females can raise up to 14 young in a single litter, the largest litter size of any marsupial.

children.name: Gracilinanus dryas
children.description: A small, delicate gracile mouse opossum of Venezuela and adjacent Colombia, named for the woodland nymphs of Greek mythology. It is known from montane cloud forest along the northern Andes and Coastal Range at elevations up to 2,000 m. Listed as Vulnerable due to its limited range and ongoing deforestation.

children.name: Gracilinanus marica
children.description: A gracile mouse opossum of dry forests and thorn scrub in Venezuela and northeastern Colombia, one of the few Gracilinanus adapted to seasonally dry habitats. It is active year-round and enters torpor during particularly harsh dry seasons. Its small size and large eyes make it an effective insectivore in the dense thorn scrub it inhabits.

children.name: Gracilinanus microtarsus
children.description: A small gracile opossum restricted to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, from São Paulo to Santa Catarina. It is associated with dense understorey vegetation and secondary forest, and has been studied as a pollinator of native Mata Atlântica plants. The name microtarsus refers to its small hind feet.

children.name: Cryptonanus
children.description: Cryptic mouse opossums — a small genus of five species recently separated from Gracilinanus on the basis of molecular and morphological evidence. Their separation was proposed in 2005 by Voss and Jansa, and the genus name reflects the cryptic, hidden nature of their distinguishing characters. They are small, arboreal, nocturnal opossums of South American grasslands, dry forest, and cerrado, and several species were previously overlooked or misidentified within museum collections.

children.name: Cryptonanus chacoensis
children.description: A small opossum of the Gran Chaco of Bolivia, Paraguay, and Argentina, one of the few small marsupials adapted to the seasonally flooded and dry thorn woodland of the Chaco. It is known from a modest number of voucher specimens and its ecology remains poorly documented. It likely enters torpor during dry season resource scarcity.

children.name: Cryptonanus guahybae
children.description: A rare gracile mouse opossum known from fewer than ten specimens collected in coastal Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil. It is listed as Critically Endangered by IUCN due to severe habitat loss in the coastal Atlantic Forest of southern Brazil. The species name refers to the Guahyba River near the type locality.

children.name: Cryptonanus unduaviensis
children.description: A gracile mouse opossum from the Yungas forest of Bolivia, described from specimens collected near the Unduavi Valley in La Paz department. It inhabits humid montane forest at elevations of 1,000–2,500 m on the eastern Andean slopes. It is the only Cryptonanus from the Andean foothills rather than lowland or dry biomes.

children.name: Tlacuatzin
children.description: Grey mouse opossums — a small Mexican genus of two species separated from Marmosa based on molecular and morphological evidence. The name derives from the Nahuatl word for opossum. They are small, nocturnal opossums of arid and semi-arid scrubland in Mexico and Central America, and their tolerance of dry conditions distinguishes them from most other mouse opossums. Females lack a pouch and carry their young attached to abdominal nipples.

children.name: Tlacuatzin canescens
children.description: The most widespread Mexican grey mouse opossum, ranging along the Pacific slope of Mexico from Sonora to Oaxaca in dry tropical forest and thorn scrub. It is notably tolerant of arid conditions and is among the few marsupials found in semi-desert habitats. It is nocturnal and arboreal, sheltering in tree cavities and dense bromeliads by day.

children.name: Tlacuatzin gaumeri
children.description: A small mouse opossum restricted to the Yucatán Peninsula of Mexico and adjacent Belize, inhabiting dry and semi-deciduous forest of the lowland tropical zone. Named in honour of American naturalist George Franklin Gaumer, who collected extensively in Yucatán in the nineteenth century. It was long treated as a subspecies of T. canescens before molecular data supported its elevation.

children.name: Lestodelphys
children.description: The Patagonian opossum — a monotypic genus and the southernmost marsupial in the world, found in the cold steppes and Monte desert of Patagonia. Lestodelphys halli is a highly carnivorous, cold-adapted opossum that stores fat in its tail for winter survival, similar to fat-tailed opossums of Thylamys. It is convergently adapted to harsh semi-arid environments and is rarely encountered, known from relatively few specimens.

children.name: Lestodelphys halli
children.description: The southernmost marsupial in the world, living in cold Patagonian steppe and Monte desert in Argentina at latitudes exceeding 50°S. It is highly carnivorous and can store fat in its tail for winter. Named in honour of American zoologist Eugene Raymond Hall, a prolific contributor to New World mammalogy.

children.name: Glironia
children.description: The bushy-tailed opossum — a monotypic genus of uncertain affinities within Didelphidae, notable for its extraordinarily bushy tail — unlike all other opossums, the tail of Glironia venusta is fully furred to the tip and used as a balancing aid rather than a prehensile organ. It inhabits the cloud forests and upper Amazon of Peru, Bolivia, and Ecuador and is among the rarest and least-known Neotropical marsupials, with very few specimens in world collections.

children.name: Glironia venusta
children.description: One of the rarest and most enigmatic Neotropical marsupials, known from scattered records in Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, and Brazil. Its fully furred, bushy tail — unique among opossums — gives it a squirrel-like appearance and is used for balance rather than grasping. It is listed as Data Deficient due to the paucity of records and lack of ecological studies.

children.name: Caluromys
children.description: Woolly opossums — large, slow-moving, arboreal opossums with exceptionally dense, soft fur and large eyes adapted for nocturnal life in the forest canopy. They are among the most arboreal of all marsupials, rarely descending to the ground. Woolly opossums are important pollinators and seed dispersers in Neotropical forest: their long snouts and brush-tipped tongues allow efficient nectar feeding, and pollen adheres to their fur during flower visits. Three species are recognised, distributed from Mexico to Bolivia.

children.name: Caluromys derbianus
children.description: The northernmost woolly opossum, ranging from Veracruz, Mexico, through Central America to Ecuador. Named in honour of Edward Henry Stanley, 13th Earl of Derby, a notable Victorian naturalist and zoo patron. It inhabits humid tropical and foothill forests, is strongly arboreal, and is an important canopy pollinator of epiphytic plants.
children.namedAfter: Edward Henry Stanley, 13th Earl of Derby

children.name: Caluromys lanatus
children.description: A large woolly opossum distributed across the Amazon basin and Andean foothills from Colombia and Venezuela south to Bolivia and Paraguay. It is notably frugivorous, consuming large quantities of ripe fruit and acting as a seed disperser for canopy trees. Its thick, woolly fur and large eyes give it a distinctive appearance among Neotropical marsupials.

children.name: Caluromys philander
children.description: A common woolly opossum of the Amazon and Guiana Shield regions, distinguishable by its relatively less furred tail compared to C. lanatus and C. derbianus. It is a canopy frugivore and nectar feeder, and has been extensively studied in French Guiana as a model for marsupial foraging ecology and home range behaviour.

children.name: Caluromysiops
children.description: The black-shouldered opossum — a monotypic genus closely related to Caluromys, distinguished by a striking black shoulder patch and restricted to the western Amazon. Like its woolly opossum relatives, Caluromysiops irrupta is highly arboreal and poorly known, with a very limited number of specimens in museum collections. It was described by Sanborn in 1951 and remains among the least-studied of all Didelphidae.

children.name: Caluromysiops irrupta
children.description: A rare and poorly known arboreal opossum of the western Amazonian lowlands of Peru and adjacent Brazil, distinguished by a bold black mantle across the shoulders. It is known from very few specimens and its ecology is almost entirely undocumented. It is presumed to be a frugivore and nectar feeder like the closely related woolly opossums.

children.name: Hyladelphys
children.description: The Kalinowski's mouse opossum — a monotypic genus of uncertain phylogenetic placement within Didelphidae, sometimes considered the most basal living didelphid. Hyladelphys kalinowskii was long known from only a handful of specimens from the Guiana Shield and western Amazon before being more recently confirmed as relatively widespread in primary lowland rainforest. Its distinctive cranial morphology and unique suite of soft-part characters set it apart from all other opossums.

children.name: Hyladelphys kalinowskii
children.description: An enigmatic, tiny opossum of primary Amazonian and Guianan rainforest, representing a distinct lineage possibly ancestral to other didelphids. Named for Polish-Peruvian naturalist Jan Sztolcman's collector Kalinowski, who made early natural history collections in Peru. Known from Peru, Bolivia, Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana, and Brazil.

children.name: Chacodelphys
children.description: The Chacoan pygmy opossum — a monotypic genus of tiny opossum endemic to the Gran Chaco of Argentina and Paraguay. Chacodelphys formosa is among the smallest marsupials in South America, weighing only 10–15 grams. It is the sole Didelphidae endemic to the Chaco biome and was formally described from Formosa Province, Argentina in 2004 from museum specimens collected decades earlier. Very little is known of its biology.

children.name: Chacodelphys formosa
children.description: One of the smallest marsupials in South America, weighing approximately 10–15 grams, and the only opossum endemic to the Gran Chaco. It was formally described in 2004 from specimens that had been housed in museum collections for decades without recognition as a distinct species. Its ecology and behaviour are almost entirely unknown.

children.name: Philander
children.description: Four-eyed opossums — medium-sized opossums named for the pale, cream-coloured supraocular spots above each eye that give the impression of an extra pair of eyes. They are arboreal and terrestrial omnivores of tropical forest and forest edges throughout Central and South America, and are among the more abundant and frequently observed opossums in Neotropical forest surveys.

children.name: Philander opossum
children.description: The most widespread four-eyed opossum, ranging from Mexico and Trinidad south to Bolivia and Brazil. Recognisable by grey upperparts, white supraocular spots, and a white-tipped tail. It inhabits tropical and subtropical forest, secondary growth, and riparian habitats up to 1,500 m. An active nocturnal predator and frugivore, it is one of the most common opossums in forest-clearing mosaics.

children.name: Philander quica
children.description: Found in the Atlantic Forest of south-eastern Brazil, Paraguay, and northern Argentina, this four-eyed opossum inhabits humid subtropical and tropical forest. It was long included within Philander opossum but is now recognised as a distinct species based on morphological and geographic grounds. It is a versatile omnivore of forest floors and lower strata, eating insects, small vertebrates, fruit, and carrion.

children.name: Philander frenatus
children.description: A distinctive four-eyed opossum of the Atlantic Forest and adjacent cerrado in south-eastern Brazil, recognised by a contrasting dark facial mask and paler overall colouration than other Philander species. It is a habitat generalist within forested landscapes and is frequently recorded in camera trap surveys of Atlantic Forest fragments. Like other four-eyed opossums it is primarily nocturnal and omnivorous.

children.name: Philander andersoni
children.description: Named after the American zoologist Sydney Anderson, this four-eyed opossum is found in the western Amazon basin of Peru, Bolivia, and adjacent Brazil. It inhabits lowland tropical rainforest, typically in terra firme and seasonally flooded forest. Darker and more richly coloured than the widespread Gray Four-eyed Opossum, it was recognised as a full species following morphological and molecular revisions of the genus.

children.name: Metachirus
children.description: The brown four-eyed opossum — a monotypic genus containing a medium-sized, terrestrially adapted opossum with pale supraocular spots. Unlike most opossums it spends most of its time on the ground rather than in trees, with a body plan more suited for running than climbing. It is one of the most insectivorous of the larger opossums and is an important predator of large invertebrates on the tropical forest floor.

children.name: Metachirus nudicaudatus
children.description: A predominantly terrestrial opossum of tropical forest from Nicaragua south to Bolivia, Paraguay, and south-eastern Brazil, characterised by brown upperparts and pale supraocular spots. It forages on the forest floor and is a powerful predator of large insects, scorpions, and small vertebrates. Unlike most opossums it is a fast runner and relies on flight rather than climbing when threatened, and seldom enters trees except to sleep.

children.name: Caluromys
children.description: Woolly opossums — large, slow-moving, strongly arboreal opossums with dense, soft fur and a prehensile tail used as an anchor in the canopy. They are among the most specialised frugivores and nectarivores in the family, with a diet dominated by soft fruit, nectar, and pollen. Their gentle manner, large eyes, and fluffy coat make them appear strikingly un-opossum-like, resembling small lemurs in the canopy.

children.name: Caluromys derbianus
children.description: Named after Edward Smith-Stanley, 13th Earl of Derby, a prominent patron of natural history, this is the largest and most widespread woolly opossum, found from Mexico south to Ecuador. It inhabits the canopy of humid tropical and montane forest and is a major pollinator of nocturnal tropical flowers, including those of balsa trees, visiting flowers regularly and transferring pollen on its fur-covered snout.

children.name: Caluromys lanatus
children.description: A large, woolly opossum of Amazonia and adjacent forests in Peru, Bolivia, Brazil, and Venezuela, inhabiting the upper and middle storeys of tropical rainforest. It moves slowly but deliberately through the canopy, using its fully prehensile tail as a safety line on narrow branches. It is an important pollinator and seed disperser in Amazonian forests, and its long hair and dense fur are unusual among Neotropical marsupials.

children.name: Caluromys philander
children.description: Found in the Guiana Shield, Trinidad, and adjacent northern Brazil and Venezuela, the Bare-tailed Woolly Opossum is distinguished by having the naked portion of its prehensile tail extending further toward the base than in other woolly opossums. It inhabits tropical lowland forest and is a nocturnal canopy specialist that feeds primarily on ripe fruit and nectar. Its populations are often associated with well-preserved primary forest.

children.name: Caluromysiops
children.description: The black-shouldered opossum — a monotypic genus closely allied to Caluromys but distinguished by bold black shoulder patches. Among the most rarely observed opossums in the wild, it is known from very few specimens and inhabits the canopy of primary rainforest in a restricted Amazonian range. It is presumed to share the frugivorous-nectarivorous habits of the woolly opossums.

children.name: Caluromysiops irrupta
children.description: One of the rarest and least known opossums, found in the western Amazon basin of Peru and adjacent Brazil. It has pale grey body fur and distinctive black patches on the shoulders, making it visually striking among Neotropical marsupials. Known from only a handful of museum specimens and occasional camera trap photographs, virtually nothing is known of its ecology or behaviour in the wild.

children.name: Glironia
children.description: The bushy-tailed opossum — a monotypic genus containing one of the most poorly known marsupials in the world. It is remarkable for having a fully furred, bushy tail — unique in the family — rather than the naked prehensile tail typical of most opossums. This suggests a different locomotor strategy and possibly a more terrestrial or trunk-climbing lifestyle.

children.name: Glironia venusta
children.description: Inhabiting the rainforests of Bolivia, Peru, Ecuador, and western Brazil, this exceptionally rare opossum is known from fewer than 30 confirmed records worldwide. Its most distinctive feature is its bushy, squirrel-like tail, covered in fur along its entire length — a trait found in no other didelphid. Camera trap images show it is arboreal and nocturnal, but its diet and social behaviour remain almost entirely unknown.

children.name: Marmosops
children.description: Slender mouse opossums — a genus of small, gracile mouse opossums with particularly long, slender limbs and narrow skulls. Like Marmosa they lack a permanent pouch, but they tend to be associated with denser, more humid forest than their relatives. They are predominantly insectivorous and inhabit the forest floor and lower strata of tropical and subtropical forest in South America.

children.name: Marmosops incanus
children.description: One of the most common and well-studied slender mouse opossums, found in the Atlantic Forest and cerrado of eastern and central Brazil. It is a frequent subject of camera trap studies and is among the most thoroughly studied Neotropical marsupials for ecology and behaviour. It is predominantly insectivorous but will also eat fruit and small vertebrates, and females carry their young clinging to the ventral fur rather than in a pouch.

children.name: Marmosops paulensis
children.description: A slender mouse opossum of the Atlantic Forest in south-eastern Brazil, particularly São Paulo state, where it inhabits humid upland and montane forest. It was long confused with the Gray Slender Mouse Opossum and separated mainly on the basis of its restricted range and higher-altitude habitat preference. Like other members of the genus it is a nocturnal insectivore of the forest understorey and floor.

children.name: Marmosops noctivagus
children.description: A widespread species of the western Amazon basin — Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, and Brazil — distinguished by its white or cream underparts contrasting with darker brownish upperparts. It inhabits lowland and foothill tropical rainforest from sea level to about 1,500 m. It is one of the most frequently encountered small marsupials in live-trap surveys of Amazonian forest and is considered a useful bioindicator of primary forest quality.

children.name: Thylamys
children.description: Fat-tailed mouse opossums — small, semi-arid-adapted opossums that store fat reserves in their tail and hindquarters during times of food abundance, drawing on these reserves during dry seasons or food scarcity. This fat storage in the tail is a remarkable convergence with the fat-tailed dunnarts of Australia. They inhabit the drier habitats of South America — the Andes, Monte Desert, Chaco, and Patagonian steppe — and are among the more cold-tolerant opossums.

children.name: Thylamys elegans
children.description: The most studied fat-tailed mouse opossum, widely distributed across the Chilean matorral shrubland and adjacent Argentina. It stores fat in its tail to survive the dry season and enters daily torpor to conserve energy. Studies have shown it can survive with a remarkably reduced metabolic rate — dropping body temperature to near-ambient — making it a model for research on marsupial thermoregulation and metabolic suppression.

children.name: Thylamys pallidior
children.description: A pale, sand-coloured fat-tailed mouse opossum of the Monte Desert and dry scrublands of Argentina, Bolivia, and Chile. Its cryptically pale fur provides camouflage in its sandy, open habitat. It is more arid-adapted than most opossums and can survive in some of the driest inhabited regions of South America. Its fat-storing tail may swell to more than twice its normal diameter during good food years.

children.name: Thylamys pusillus
children.description: One of the smaller members of the genus, inhabiting the dry Chaco scrubland of Bolivia, Paraguay, and Argentina. It occupies xeric woodland and thornscrub habitats and is primarily insectivorous and frugivorous. Like other Thylamys it accumulates subcutaneous fat in its tail during the wet season, drawing on this reserve to survive the harsh dry season when invertebrate prey becomes scarce.

children.name: Thylamys sponsorius
children.description: Found in the monte and prepuna (dry rocky uplands) of north-western Argentina, particularly in the provinces of Salta and Tucumán, at elevations of 800 to 3,000 m. It inhabits thornscrub and rocky outcrops and is one of the higher-altitude members of the genus. It is poorly known but is presumed to have the same fat-storing and torpor capacities as other Thylamys species, which would be particularly valuable at altitude.

children.name: Thylamys venustus
children.description: Inhabiting the Andean prepuna and puna grasslands of Bolivia and Argentina at elevations typically above 2,000 m, this high-altitude opossum has longer, denser fur than most of its relatives — an adaptation to colder montane conditions. It is among the most cold-tolerant members of the family and has been recorded at elevations above 3,500 m, making it one of the highest-dwelling marsupials in South America.

children.name: Thylamys macrurus
children.description: The largest member of the genus, characterised by a proportionally longer tail relative to body length, found in the Cerrado and transitional forests of central and western Brazil, Paraguay, and Bolivia. It inhabits dry deciduous woodland, thornscrub, and gallery forest, and its tail may reach 140% of body length — impressively long even among small marsupials. It is considered near threatened due to rapid loss of Cerrado habitat.

children.name: Thylamys tatei
children.description: Named after the American zoologist George Henry Hamilton Tate, who made extensive mammal collections in South America in the 1930s–1940s, this fat-tailed mouse opossum is known from a restricted area of the Bolivian Andes. It inhabits cloud forest and transitional zones between montane forest and puna grassland at elevations of 2,000–3,500 m. Its ecology is poorly documented and most records come from museum specimens collected by Tate's expeditions.

children.name: Cryptonanus
children.description: Gracile mouse opossums — a recently described genus separated from Gracilinanus on the basis of molecular evidence, containing small, finely-built mouse opossums of South American open and transitional habitats. They differ from Gracilinanus in karyotype and molecular markers but are morphologically very similar, and their taxonomy continues to be refined as new specimens and data become available.

children.name: Cryptonanus chacoensis
children.description: A small, gracile mouse opossum of the Gran Chaco — the dry thornscrub and woodland of Bolivia, Paraguay, Argentina, and adjacent Brazil. It is one of the most commonly encountered small marsupials in live-trap surveys of the Chaco and tolerates disturbed habitats better than most relatives. It is an important insectivore and occasional frugivore in the Chaco ecosystem.

children.name: Cryptonanus guahybae
children.description: Known only from a handful of specimens from Rio Grande do Sul state in southern Brazil, this small mouse opossum inhabits the transitional forest-grassland ecotones of south Brazil's subtropical zone. It is one of the least-known marsupials on the continent, and its conservation status is uncertain given the rapid conversion of its native grassland and forest habitats to agriculture in southern Brazil.

children.name: Cryptonanus agricolai
children.description: A gracile mouse opossum of the dry cerrado and caatinga biomes of north-eastern and central Brazil, inhabiting thornscrub, dry woodland, and the ecotones between these habitats. It is primarily insectivorous but consumes fruit seasonally. Camera trap studies in the caatinga have revealed it to be more common than museum records suggest, often recorded in scrubby habitats near seasonal water sources.

children.name: Gracilinanus
children.description: Gracile mouse opossums — very small, delicate mouse opossums of forest and woodland habitats in South America. Distinguished from other small opossums by their particularly fine-boned, gracile skull and limb structure, large ears, and prominent eyes. They are among the most arboreal of the small opossums, foraging in the lower and middle strata of forest, and are important predators of small insects and consumers of small fruit.

children.name: Gracilinanus agilis
children.description: The most widespread and abundant gracile mouse opossum, found across much of central and eastern South America from Colombia to Argentina in cerrado, Chaco, and transitional forest. It is a highly agile climber, moving rapidly through shrubs and lower forest strata in search of insects, spiders, and small berries. It is an important pollinator of some tropical flowers and is frequently the most abundant small marsupial in cerrado surveys.

children.name: Gracilinanus microtarsus
children.description: A small mouse opossum of the Atlantic Forest in south-eastern Brazil, found from Bahia to Rio Grande do Sul. It is associated with humid montane and submontane forest and is more forest-dependent than the Agile Gracile Mouse Opossum. It is an important prey item for small owls and raptors in the Atlantic Forest, and its presence is considered an indicator of forest quality.

children.name: Gracilinanus aceramarcae
children.description: Known from cloud forest in the Yungas of Bolivia and adjacent Peru, this high-altitude gracile mouse opossum inhabits dense montane forest between 1,500 and 2,800 m. It was described from specimens collected near the town of Aceramarca in La Paz Department. Like other montane marsupials it has longer and denser fur than its lowland relatives, and its ecology in Andean cloud forest remains poorly studied.

children.name: Hyladelphys
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Kalinowski's Mouse Opossum, which was placed in its own genus following molecular phylogenetic analysis that showed it was not closely related to any other mouse opossum genus. It differs from other small opossums in several cranial and dental features and is known only from the humid lowland forests of the Guiana Shield and western Amazon.

children.name: Hyladelphys kalinowskii
children.description: Named after the Polish naturalist Jan Kalinowski who collected widely in South America in the late 19th century, this small and rarely encountered opossum inhabits the humid lowland rainforests of the Guiana Shield, Venezuela, Guyana, Suriname, and adjacent Brazil. Known from very few specimens, it is an enigmatic species whose ecology and behaviour in the wild are virtually unstudied. Its phylogenetic isolation makes it of particular interest for understanding marsupial evolution.

children.name: Lestodelphys
children.description: The Patagonian opossum — a monotypic genus and the world's southernmost marsupial. It is unique among opossums in being adapted to cold temperate and semi-arid conditions, with a distinctly more carnivorous diet than its relatives. Its thick fur and fat stores allow it to survive the harsh winters of Patagonia, and it enters torpor during the coldest months.

children.name: Lestodelphys halli
children.description: Named after the American mammalogist E. Raymond Hall, this is the world's southernmost marsupial, inhabiting the cold semi-arid steppes of Patagonia in Argentina. It is remarkable among opossums for its distinctly carnivorous diet — a high proportion of birds, lizards, and other small vertebrates — and for surviving at latitudes and temperatures that would be lethal to other members of the family. It stores fat in its tail during autumn to fuel torpor through the Patagonian winter.

children.name: Lutreolina
children.description: The thick-tailed opossum (comadreja colorada) — a monotypic genus of robust, weasel-like opossums with short limbs, a stocky build, and a notably thick, muscular tail. It is a specialised predator of small mammals, birds, and reptiles and is the most carnivorous of all opossums. Its body plan and hunting behaviour parallel those of mustelids (weasels and ferrets), and it is sometimes called the lutrine opossum for this resemblance.

children.name: Lutreolina crassicaudata
children.description: A weasel-like opossum with orange-brown fur, a low-slung body, and a thick, muscular tail, found in grasslands, marshes, forest edges, and riparian habitats from Venezuela and Trinidad south to Argentina. The most carnivorous didelphid, it actively hunts rodents, small birds, lizards, and frogs, overpowering prey much larger than itself. It is a strong swimmer and frequently hunts in marshy habitats.

children.name: Tlacuatzin
children.description: The gray mouse opossum (tlacuache de abuela) — a monotypic genus found only in Mexico, separated from Marmosa on the basis of its distinctive karyotype (2n=14, the lowest chromosome number in the family) and molecular data. It inhabits the dry tropical forests and scrublands of Mexico's Pacific coast and is named with a word from the Nahuatl language of the Aztecs.

children.name: Tlacuatzin canescens
children.description: Endemic to Mexico, where it inhabits the dry tropical forests, thornscrub, and deciduous woodland of the Pacific coastal lowlands from Sinaloa to Oaxaca. It is distinguished from other mouse opossums by its pale grey, grizzled fur and unique chromosome number of 2n=14. An important nocturnal insectivore and pollinator in its dry forest ecosystem, it is considered near threatened due to ongoing habitat loss along Mexico's Pacific coast.
